question: What is the purpose of the following code snippet? Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in); int age = scanner.nextInt();
option_a: To read a double value from the user
option_b: To read a boolean value from the user
option_c: To read a string from the user
option_d: To read an integer value from the user
description: scanner.nextInt() reads the next token from input and parses it as an int. For other types you'd use nextDouble(), nextBoolean(), or next()/nextLine() for strings.

question: What type of error does this code generate? public static void main(String[] args) { int x; System.out.println(x); }
option_a: No error; x is used correctly
option_b: Syntax error: variable x might not have been initialized
description: Java local variables have no default value. The compiler refuses to use them until definitely assigned, producing the error: variable x might not have been initialized.

question: What is printed by the following code? for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { for (int j = 0; j < 2; j++) { System.out.print(i + " " + j + " "); } System.out.println(); }
option_d: Nested loop output with 3 rows and 2 columns
description: Outer loop i=0..2, inner j=0..1. Each inner iteration prints i j . After the inner loop a newline is printed. Combined linearly the values are: 0 0 0 1 1 0 1 1 2 0 2 1.

question: What error will this code produce? public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello World"); } }
option_d: No error; it compiles and runs fine
description: This is a perfectly valid Java program. It compiles and prints Hello World.

question: Which of these is the correct way to declare a constant in Java?
description: Java uses the final keyword to declare constants. const is reserved but unused, and immutable isn't a Java keyword.

question: What does this code output if the user inputs "5 3 0"? Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int sum = 0, num; while ((num = sc.nextInt()) != 0) { sum += num; } System.out.println(sum);
description: Reads 5 → sum=5. Reads 3 → sum=8. Reads 0 → loop exits (since 0 != 0 is false). Final output: 8.

question: Given the following code, how many times will "Java" be printed? for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { if (i % 2 == 0) { System.out.println("Java"); } }
description: i % 2 == 0 is true for the even values 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 — that's 5 times out of 10 iterations.

question: How can you create an infinite for loop in Java?
description: for (;;) is the canonical infinite loop — no init, no condition (treated as true), no update. Note: option B is also infinite-ish until int overflow, but the standard idiom is for (;;).

question: Which of the following code is used to create a Scanner object for reading user input in Java?
description: The standard input stream in Java is System.in. The Scanner class wraps it to parse primitives and strings.

question: What will be the output of this code snippet? int x = 10; int y = x++; System.out.println(x + ", " + y);
description: Post-increment (x++) returns the old value before incrementing. So y = 10, then x becomes 11. Output: 11, 10.

question: Java's platform independence is due to the JVM?
description: True. Java compiles to bytecode, which the JVM on each platform executes — this is the foundation of WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere).

question: What is the output of the following code? int a = 5, b = 10; if (a < b && a++ < b) { a += 2; } System.out.println(a);
description: a<b is true (5<10). Then a++ < b evaluates: 5<10 true and a becomes 6 (post-increment side effect). Block runs: a += 2 → a = 8.

question: What type of language is Java?
description: Java is both: source code is compiled to bytecode by javac, and the bytecode is interpreted (or JIT-compiled) by the JVM at runtime.

question: Which of the following best describes the JRE?
option_a: A package of tools for developing Java applications
option_b: The virtual machine that runs bytecode
option_c: A program that compiles Java source code into bytecode
option_d: The environment for running Java applications
description: JRE (Java Runtime Environment) is the environment for running Java apps (JVM + libraries). The JDK is for development, and the JVM is just the virtual machine inside the JRE.

question: Given the following code, what is printed? int a = 5, b = 8, c = 12; if (a > b || b < c && a < c) { System.out.println("Condition met"); } else { System.out.println("Condition not met"); }
description: && binds tighter than ||, so the expression is: a>b || (b<c && a<c) → false || (true && true) → true. Prints Condition met.

question: What is the output of the following code snippet? float f = 12.5F; double d = f; System.out.println(d);
description: Widening conversion from float to double is implicit and safe. println(double) prints 12.5 without the F suffix.

question: Which component is responsible for executing Java bytecode?
description: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the component that loads and executes bytecode. JRE is the bundle that includes the JVM + libraries; JDK adds dev tools.

question: Which of the following statements is true about the switch statement?
option_a: switch must include a default case
option_b: All cases must have a break
option_c: switch can evaluate any data type
option_d: switch can only evaluate int, char, String, or enum
description: Java's switch accepts byte, short, int, char, String, and enum (not floats, doubles, or booleans). default is optional and break isn't required (omitting causes fall-through).

question: What will this code output? int x = 4, y = 5; if (x > y || ++x == y) { y++; } System.out.println(x + ", " + y);
description: x>y false (4>5). Then ++x==y evaluates: pre-increment makes x=5, then 5==5 is true. Block runs → y becomes 6. Output: 5, 6.

question: What will be printed if x = 3 and y = 4 in the following code? if (x < y) { if (y > 2) { System.out.println("A"); } else { System.out.println("B"); } } else { System.out.println("C"); }
description: x<y (3<4) is true → enter outer block. y>2 (4>2) is true → prints A. (Note: option C in this question is the letter "A", which is the correct printed value.)
